ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle dish class; no miso-specific ramen subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a miso ramen-specific code. A0FKP (noodle soup dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for miso ramen as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of miso ramen is approximately 500 g, comprising noodles, broth, and toppings such as chashu pork, fishcake, preserved radish, and seaweed.

nutrient_source.title: Miso Ramen — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Miso Ramen (cr_id F05060090). Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for ramen noodle soup (NDB No. 22899) as an approximate supplement. Iron reported as -1 in source (data not available in HPB record).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for miso ramen as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database convention) based on the GI of wheat-flour noodles (ramen-type, GI ~52–58) as reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (43.4 g from 500 g serving at 8.68 g/100g) ÷ 100 = 23.9, rounded to 24.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 310 mg/100 g; a standard 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1550 mg sodium, representing 67–78% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit miso ramen to occasional consumption, request reduced-sodium broth, and avoid drinking the remaining broth to substantially reduce sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles with low-to-moderate GI (~55) and a high carbohydrate load per serving (~43 g per 500 g bowl) can cause meaningful post-prandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce noodle portion size, request whole-grain or konjac noodle substitutes where available, and increase protein and vegetable toppings to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (310 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from chashu pork contribute to cardiovascular risk; a full serving delivers approximately 1550 mg sodium and moderate saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose leaner protein toppings in place of chashu pork, consume only half the broth, and avoid adding soy sauce or seasoning to keep sodium and saturated fat within safe limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chashu pork (red meat) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways; refined wheat noodles provide low fibre and high refined carbohydrate, both of which are pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute chashu pork with tofu or egg, add extra vegetables such as spinach and mushrooms to increase anti-inflammatory polyphenols and fibre, and note that the fermented miso component alone is insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory profile of the full dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Miso ramen (味噌ラーメン, Miso Rāmen) is a Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of springy wheat-flour ramen noodles served in a rich, savoury broth made from fermented soybean paste (miso) combined with pork or chicken stock. The Singapore-style preparation described here is topped with sliced chashu pork (braised pork belly), fishcakes, preserved radish (takuan), and dried seaweed (nori). The broth is umami-forward, mildly salty, and slightly sweet from the miso fermentation. The noodles are chewy and dense. Per 100 g, the dish provides 57 kcal, 3.13 g protein, 1.11 g fat, and 8.68 g carbohydrate, with 310 mg sodium — a clinically significant sodium level given typical serving sizes of 400–600 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low by University of Sydney GI Database convention, GI ≤55), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 24 per standard 500 g bowl. Miso ramen is not suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its high sodium load per serving, and requires careful portion management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its refined carbohydrate content. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal due to pork content. It contains gluten, soy, and fish allergens.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in miso ramen?
Is miso ramen high in carbs?
What is the calorie count of miso ramen?
Is miso ramen suitable for hypertension?
What are the ingredients in miso ramen?
What is the GI of ramen noodles?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060090, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as an approximate estimate. GI estimated from peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2021) using the closest analogous food (wheat-flour ramen noodles). All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: iron_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo source record for Miso Ramen (cr_id F05060090) does not report iron content. No supplementary iron value was available from USDA FoodData Central for an exact miso ramen composite match. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile for this composite miso ramen dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. saturated_fat_g (0.35 g/100g) is an approximate estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for ramen noodle soup as a supplementary source, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. Cultural metadata corrected: input record incorrectly classified primary_cuisine as 'western'; corrected to 'Japanese' based on dish origin. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour ramen noodles (GI ~55) per Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for miso ramen as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. gi_category corrected to 'low': per University of Sydney GI Database convention, GI ≤55 is classified as low; the previous iteration incorrectly assigned 'medium' to GI 55.
